title: Iron water mordant method
subtitle: Making and using an iron water modifier from rusty iron and vinegar to shift and sadden natural dyes
excerpt: Iron water is a dilute iron solution made by soaking rusty iron nails or pieces in water and vinegar for several days. Used as an after-bath or modifier, it darkens and saddens natural dye colours toward green or charcoal. It is a lower-hazard alternative to bought iron sulphate for small-scale home dyeing.
sourceNotes: Iron water as a dye modifier documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in contemporary natural-dye teaching. The technique of using ferrous water from rusty iron as a colour modifier is traditional in home dyeing practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dilute ferrous solution used as an after-bath to darken and sadden natural dye colours. Iron shifts yellow toward green and olive, shifts orange toward brown, and shifts most colours toward a cooler, more muted version.
The deliberate darkening or dulling of a natural dye colour using an iron modifier or iron after-bath. Saddened colours are more muted and earthy than the undarkened original.
The dye liquid in which the mordanted fibre is placed. In this technique, a second bath of iron water is used after the primary dye bath to modify the colour.

content.text: Iron water is a straightforward way to introduce
without buying iron sulphate. The homemade solution is more dilute and slower-acting than iron sulphate, which makes it easier to use without over-darkening the
on already-dyed fibre, not as a primary mordant.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Iron water-modified dye baths go down a foul drain only. Never into a storm drain, watercourse, or compost heap.

items.name: Old rusty iron nails or small iron pieces

content.text: Place rusty nails or iron fragments in a glass jar. Cover with equal parts white vinegar and water. Leave to soak for 3 to 7 days; the liquid will turn a brownish orange as iron leaches into the vinegar solution. Strain through a cloth into a clean bottle; the iron water can be stored in a closed glass bottle for several months. Label the bottle clearly as dye-only and store away from food.
Using iron water as an after-bath'

Once the primary dye is done and the fibre has been rinsed, make an after-bath in the dye-pot: add 100 ml of iron water to 2 litres of warm water at 50 to 60 degrees Celsius. Immerse the rinsed, damp fibre in the iron water bath for 5 to 15 minutes. Check the colour shift every 5 minutes; iron water is gentle enough to allow incremental colour control. Remove the fibre when the desired saddening is reached and rinse thoroughly.

Yellow dyes (fennel, chamomile, onion) shift toward yellow-green or olive. Orange-brown dyes (walnut, onion with iron) shift toward deeper brown. Blue-green results are achievable from most flavonoid yellows combined with iron water.

Iron water modifier baths must go down the foul drain only. Never pour iron-containing dye waste into a storm drain, watercourse, or garden soil. Iron is toxic to aquatic organisms at higher concentrations.

items.problem: The iron water is not producing any colour shift
items.cause: The iron water has not concentrated sufficiently; the nails were not rusty enough or the soak time was too short
items.fix: Leave the nails to soak for a full week and use iron that shows visible surface rust. Alternatively, increase the vinegar proportion to 3 parts vinegar to 1 part water for faster iron extraction.

items.problem: The fibre has gone a very dark, uneven grey rather than the olive-green expected
items.cause: The fibre was left in the iron bath too long or the iron water was too concentrated
items.fix: Iron water from rusty nails is uncontrolled in concentration. Use small quantities (50 ml per 2 litres) and check every 5 minutes. Once the fibre has over-saddened, rinse immediately in slightly warm water with a few drops of vinegar to help slow the reaction.
